dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 4 responding for breathing problems in the [CITY] area.
Hi, I'm Alex from the rescue squad. What's the reason for your 911 call today?
I can't breathe. It started about an hour ago when I got back home from the hospital.
Mr. Smith, can you tell me your full name and where you are right now?
Good. What is today's date and what year are we in?
And can you describe what happened earlier at the hospital?
To check your motor function, if you can squeeze both of my hands, one at a time, that would help.
Your airway is clear and you are maintaining it yourself.
You are breathing 36 times a minute, which is fast and with extra effort, using accessory muscles.
Your pulse is strong and regular.

dialogue.micro_intent: Oxygen saturation measured with pulse oximetry
dialogue.utterance: Oxygen saturation is 90%, slightly below normal.
Your blood pressure is 170 over 90 now.
When did you first notice this shortness of breath today?
Was there a reason you were at the hospital this morning?
Your chart shows a history of congestive heart failure and COPD. Any other medical conditions I should be aware of?
I am going to check your head, eyes, ears, nose, and throat.
You are alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. The trachea is in the middle line with no signs of jugular venous distension.
Now I will listen to your chest. Can you take a few deep breaths in for me?
Lung sounds are clear and equal bilaterally.
I'll now check your abdomen, which appears soft and non-tender to palpation.
No new trauma observed. I note an amputation on your left lower limb. Your back appears normal.
Pulse was 84 at first check.
Let me attach the EKG leads for a heart rhythm evaluation.
Oxygen saturation improved following treatment to 100%.
Blood pressure has changed to 156 over 81.
We are giving you 2 liters of oxygen through your nose to ease your breathing.
We've placed a 4-lead EKG to monitor your heart function continuously.
A 12-lead ECG has been placed and results are being reviewed.
BP is now 156 over 81, heart rate is 98 with good oxygenation at 100%.
We are transporting you to [COMPANY_NAME] Green ED. That is your preferred hospital, correct?
We now need to move you from your wheelchair to the stretcher.
I will use a stair chair to assist with the transfer to keep it safe.
When you're on the stretcher, we will secure you in a semi-fowler's position for comfort.
I will now begin moving you from the wheelchair to the stair chair.
You are now on the stretcher and secured with five straps. That all right for you?
Respiratory rate is now 28 and regular.
As we approach the ambulance, one of us will remain with you continuously.
Oxygen saturation is unchanged at 100% and heart rate is 98.
We'll now place you into the ambulance for transport to the hospital.
Oxygen remains at 2 liters and EKG rhythm is stable and shows a paced pattern.
No current allergies listed. Were you known to have any allergies early today at the hospital?
You are on regular medications including Lasix and Metoprolol. Did you get a different dose or combination at the hospital?
Your pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light. No swelling or lumps noted.
Once at the hospital, you will be transferred using a lateral slide onto the bed.
Your medical record number will be noted and given to the hospital team.
With improved breathing, anything else that you want to add about your condition?
EKG reviewed again and confirms a regular paced rhythm with no new findings.
You're now on a hospital bed and attended by the nursing staff on arrival.
We have handed you over to the ED team at [COMPANY_NAME] Green ED, bed number 14.
Final signs show 100% oxygen saturation, 98 bpm, and blood pressure 156/81.
Call transfer has been successfully completed with hospital personnel.
Were you given any food or drink at the hospital? When was the last time you had something?

dialogue.micro_intent: events leading up to the event
dialogue.utterance: As you were coming home from the hospital, when did shortness of breath become severe?
Chest sounds and rhythm look stable with no irregularities.
No injuries or issues identified in the HEENT region. You can swallow and are keeping yourself comfortable.
No contusions or tenderness observed in the chest area upon feeling.
Pelvis remains steady and non-tender with no movement-related symptoms.
No glucose found at the start and a second check was not necessary after O2 improved breathing.
Your history of CHF, COPD, and amputation will be noted for the hospital staff.
Your skin appears normal with no rashes or unusual markings.
Pain has remained consistent at zero throughout, as previously reported.
[COMPANY_NAME] is the destination. No other facility was requested.
We will complete documentation and wrap up the call now.
